title: Floquet quantum multiparameter estimation with periodic-driving-induced topological phase transition
authors: Yu Yang, Yuyang Tang, Pei Zhang, Fuli Li
abstract: Periodically driven systems provide a powerful platform for quantum multiparameter estimation. Constructing a static effective Hamiltonian in a proper rotating frame is commonly employed to assess the attainable precision. However, such an approach becomes nonfeasible for more general time-periodically driven systems. To tackle this dilemma, we develop a quantum multiparameter estimation strategy in the Floquet theory framework. The contributions of Floquet eigenmodes, quasienergies, and multi-photon processes to the quantum Fisher information matrix and measurement incompatibility are determined, respectively. Moreover, this approach is applied to a ring-shaped Rashba spin-orbit interferometer model exhibiting the topological phase transition (TPT). In the vicinity of the TPT boundary, we reveal a pronounced enhancement in the estimation precision of multiple parameters with the Heisenberg limit scaling and even higher. Meanwhile, the measurement incompatibility vanishes in an oscillatory manner, and the stroboscopic projective measurement enables the highest estimation precision achievable. This work provides a complete Floquet picture for time-dependent critical quantum multiparameter estimation.
summary: This paper presents a new strategy for simultaneous multi-parameter estimation in time-periodic quantum systems using Floquet theory. It demonstrates that operating near a topological phase transition significantly enhances sensing precision, even achieving super-Heisenberg scaling for the driving frequency.
main_problem: Developing a quantum multiparameter estimation strategy for general time-periodically driven (Floquet) systems where a static effective Hamiltonian cannot be easily constructed.
main_result: The study reveals that near a periodic-driving-induced topological phase transition, estimation precision for magnetic fields reaches the Heisenberg limit, while the frequency parameter can achieve even higher super-Heisenberg scaling (t^4).
method: The authors utilize Floquet theory within the Sambe space expansion and exact diagonalization of a truncated Floquet Hamiltonian to decompose the Quantum Fisher Information Matrix into contributions from eigenmodes, quasienergies, and multi-photon processes.
model_or_system: A ring-shaped Rashba spin-orbit interferometer (modeled as a 2-level system) subject to a time-periodic Hamiltonian involving Rashba and Zeeman magnetic fields and a driving frequency.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information Matrix (QFIM), Classical Fisher Information (CFI), winding number, measurement incompatibility, and transition probabilities.
important_parameters: Rashba field (B0), Zeeman field (B1), driving frequency (omega), Heisenberg limit (t^2), and super-Heisenberg scaling (t^4).
main_assumptions: The approach assumes the system can be effectively modeled by a truncated Floquet matrix in Sambe space and focuses on the stroboscopic measurement regime.
figures_summary: Schematic of the interferometer, winding number plots, energy spectra near TPT, QFI scaling analysis over time, decomposition of QFI components, and measurement incompatibility plots.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Floquet multiparameter estimation, develops a mathematical framework using Floquet theory and Sambe space, applies this to a Rashba spin-orbit interferometer undergoing a topological phase transition, analyzes precision scaling and measurement incompatibility, and concludes with experimental feasibility mapping.
why_interesting: It provides a novel framework for quantum sensing in non-equilibrium driven systems, demonstrating how topological phase transitions can be leveraged to surpass standard precision limits in multi-parameter estimation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Ring-shaped Rashba spin-orbit interferometer. The system is subjected to an xy-plane Rashba magnetic field B0 and a Zeeman field B1 along the x-direction. The motion of electrons accompanies the spin precession. (b) Winding number nK of equation (36) versus the parameters B0 and B1. (c) Instantaneous energy spectra of the Hamiltonian (30) during three time periods. The top and bottom halves re- spectively show the behavior of the instantaneous eigenval- ues E± = ± √
FIG. 2. Contourplot of the total phase of equation (47). The red line indicates the boundary of the topology phase transi- tion.

FIG. 3. (a)-(c) QFIs IB0, IB1, Iω for the parameters B0, B1, and ω versus B0 and B1. The insets in panels (a)-(c) illustrate the behavior of IB0, IB1, and Iω both away from and near the TPT. (d)-(f) QFIs IB0, IB1, and Iω versus the time t over one time period for the fixed TPT condition (B0 = B1 = 0.5). The standard quantum limit (SQL) ∼t, the Heisenberg limit (HL) ∼t2, and the scaling of ∼t4 are also exhibited for the comparison.

FIG. 4. (a)-(c) Total QFI versus its Floquet-resolved components (eigenmodes, quasienergies, multi-photon processes, and coherence) for the parameters B0, B1, and ω with the fixed B1 = 5. The TPT region around B0 = B1 = 5 is indicated by the shaded area.
FIG. 5. (a)-(c) Comparison of the QFI (blue surface) and its upper bound (yellow surface) for the parameters B0, B1, and ω. These two surfaces are overlapped at the TPT.
